title: Reading and Use of English – Part 1
description: For questions 1–8, read the text below and choose which answer (A, B, C or D) best completes each gap.

content.text: Radio was one of the most revolutionary inventions of the early 20th century, (0) the way people received news and entertainment. Before television, families would (1) around the radio to listen to music, dramas, and news broadcasts. The technology was developed by several inventors, though Guglielmo Marconi is often (2) with its creation. The first commercial radio stations began broadcasting in the 1920s, and the medium quickly (3) popularity. During World War II, radio played a crucial (4) in spreading information and boosting morale. The invention of the transistor made radios smaller and portable, allowing people to take them anywhere. When television emerged, many predicted that radio would (5) out completely. However, radio adapted by focusing on music programming and talk shows. Today, despite competition from streaming services and podcasts, traditional radio continues to (6) millions of listeners worldwide. Internet radio has also opened up new possibilities, allowing anyone to (7) their own station. The medium has proven remarkably resilient, demonstrating that there will always be a (8) for audio entertainment.

example.explanation: 'Transforming' is the most appropriate verb to describe a fundamental and complete change.

questions.explanation: 'Gather around' is the correct phrasal verb to describe people gathering around something.
'Credited with' is the correct expression to attribute an achievement to someone.
'Gained popularity' is the standard collocation to express that something has become popular.
'Play a crucial role' is a fixed expression that means to play an important role.
'Fade out' is the correct phrasal verb to describe something that gradually disappears.
'Attract listeners' is the correct collocation to describe how a medium attracts an audience.
'Create a station' is the most natural expression to describe the beginning of a station.
'Demand for' is the correct collocation to refer to the demand for something.
